text:
PARIS 芒聙聰 It芒聙聶s important to see the sites when you are traveling, so after checking out the Eiffel Tower and the Picasso Museum, I recently made my way to my favorite Paris landmark: the American food section of the city芒聙聶s fanciest grocery store.脗

La Grande 脙聣picerie, about a mile east of where archery is being contested at the complex that houses Napoleon芒聙聶s tomb, is the grocery store attached to Le Bon March脙漏, a high-end department store owned by LVMH, which also owns Louis Vuitton, Moet-Hennessy and Tiffany.

La Grande 脙聣picerie mostly matches its LVMH siblings in luxury, from the multiple foie gras refrigerators to the cheese counter that almost needs its own postcode. Near the produce stands a series of shelves housing international foods. Italy: pasta, tomato sauce, biscotti. Japan: nori, rice crackers, miso paste. Canada: maple syrup, maple jelly, maple sugar.脗

In the center lurks the United States, represented here by what the French seem to believe to be our national delicacies: Reese芒聙聶s, marshmallow fluff and two varieties of corn syrup. There are also half a dozen types of microwave popcorn, sour candy, off-brand boxed macaroni and cheese, jars of 芒聙聹hot-dog sausages芒聙聺 and Hershey芒聙聶s cookies and cream bars.脗

Again, this is a gourmet grocery store.脗

As an American, I am offended. As a journalist, I am intrigued.脗

I stop by this area nearly every time I am in the city, hoping to see someone purchase something so I can ask why, but I have never been lucky. Until today, when within a few minutes of lurking I caught sight of a young French woman plucking a package of Reese芒聙聶s dipped pretzels from the shelf. I pounced.脗

芒聙聹It芒聙聶s a mix of sweet and salty, and Americans love sweet and salty,芒聙聺 Juliette Sabater, who works in marketing, explains in French. Most of what is on offer here is too sweet for her, she adds.

Yes, that is our primary reputation abroad, and it芒聙聶s earned:Accordingto a 2015 study by the market research firm Euromonitor, Americans average more than 126 grams of sugar per day, nearly twice the French intake芒聙聰and more than twice the World Health Organization芒聙聶s recommendation.脗

We are perhaps not helping our cause right now with the Krispy Kreme ads throughout the Paris Metro system that bear a photo of a donut with a bite taken out of it, making it look crescent-shaped, and the caption:The best croissant in Paris.

Meanwhile, two American tourists, teachers in New Jersey, are walking by and chuckling about the selection. 芒聙聹It芒聙聶s all crap,芒聙聺 says Michael Fleming with a laugh. He adds that when he spent time in the United Kingdom, he noticed that locals were very interested in chocolate syrup and brown sugar. But at least those are ingredients. These are finished products, although it芒聙聶s hard to imagine finishing some of them.脗

芒聙聹I think it芒聙聶s kind of strange,芒聙聺 says Katie Laurino. 芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t wish I had some 芒聙聵hot-dog sausages芒聙聶 in a jar.芒聙聺

C脙漏cile Irtelli, who helps select the products for the U.K. section芒聙聰mostly teas, although they also get a dozen varieties of shortbread and a few other desserts芒聙聰insists that that attitude is the minority.脗

芒聙聹These are very popular,芒聙聺 she says in French. 芒聙聹We have French people who come from all over France. You can芒聙聶t find these products elsewhere.芒聙聺 Beef jerky, she says, drives people especially mad. 芒聙聹We don芒聙聶t have it at all in France.芒聙聺

French people understand that there is more to American food than partially hydrogenated soybean oil, explains Christy Shields, an associate professor of anthropology at the American University of Paris who specializes in the study of food. But 芒聙聹you still have the stereotype that Americans eat a bunch of junk and they eat a lot of it.芒聙聺 (Speaking of stereotypes, I also emailed an associate professor who teaches a food culture class at the Sorbonne. Her autoreply promised to get back to me some time in September.)

As more French people travel to the United States芒聙聰at any given moment there seems to be more French than English spoken in Central Park芒聙聰they return with an appreciation for what they have eaten there, and they bring it back with them. 芒聙聹There are a growing number of Mexican-American restaurants,芒聙聺 Shields says. 芒聙聹There芒聙聶s a fascination with soul food. There are burgers in every restaurant and caf脙漏 now.芒聙聺 And another American import has taken hold: 芒聙聹Oh my God,芒聙聺 she says. 芒聙聹The brunch.芒聙聺

Some of our cultural quirks have been less successful. I tell Irtelli the story of when I studied abroad here and my host family welcomed me with a small jar of peanut butter (bought at a sadly defunct American food store called Thanksgiving). Their only request: that I not defile their jelly with it. Irtelli gasps at the very idea.脗

But Shields was delighted when she recently learned of a store a mile and a half east of La Grande 脙聣picerie called COW: Cheese of the World. It stocks a wonderful ch脙篓vre from Vermont. 芒聙聹I was so happy to find an American product that was artisanal,芒聙聺 she says.脗

Those are in short supply at La Grande 脙聣picerie. The closest thing I find to a mom-and-pop producer is Paul Newman芒聙聶s ranch dressing. While I am investigating it, Sabater reconsiders her purchase. She returns the Reese芒聙聶s pretzels. Instead she selects what appears to me to be an almost identical product by Flipz芒聙聰from the U.K. section.脗

Stephanie Apstein is a senior writer covering baseball and Olympic sports for Sports Illustrated, where she started as an intern in 2011. She has covered 10 World Series and three Olympics, and is a frequent contributor to SportsNet New York's Baseball Night in New York. Apstein has twice won top honors from the Associated Press Sports Editors, and her work has been included in the Best American Sports Writing book series. A member of the Baseball Writers Association of America who serves as its New York chapter vice chair, she graduated from Trinity College with a bachelor's in French and Italian, and has a master's in journalism from Columbia University.

title: This Is What the American Section of a Fancy French Grocery Store Looks Like
description: Everyone knows hauling grocery bags can be an Olympic sport. In between competitions in Paris, we paid a visit to the aisle that stocks what France considers to be the U.S.芒聙聶s national delicacies.
